channel.title: People on IQSS Clone - Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science
channel.description: Recent content in People on IQSS Clone - Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science

item.title: Len Wisniewski
item.description:
Len Wisniewski

Senior Director of Research Computing
email lwisniewski@iq.harvard.edu

Len Wisniewski is Senior Director of Research Computing at IQSS.  Before IQSS, Len was Engineering Manager for the High Performance Computing group at Sun Microsystems, leading the teams developing the Sun HPC ClusterTools and Sun Studio Performance Analyzer products.   In that role, he actively supported the Open MPI open-source community.   Before entering a management role, Len held software engineering roles at various companies, including Sun/Oracle, Acopia Networks (acquired by F5 Networks), and Thinking Machines (acquired by Sun).  His primary technical interests have been in developing algorithms and file systems for parallel and distributed computers.

item.title: Zoe Szajnfarber
item.description:
Zoe Szajnfarber

Visiting Scholar, Laboratory for Innovation Science (LISH)
Associate Professor of Engineering Management and Systems Engineering and of International Affairs at the George Washington University
email zszajnfarber@fas.harvard.edu

Zoe Szajnfarber is an Associate Professor of Engineering Management and Systems Engineering and of International Affairs at the George Washington University. Her research focuses on design and innovation management in complex systems, with particular emphasis on how the organizational and technical systems architectures interact, and affect engineering work, during the design and development process. She has spent most of her career studying the aerospace and defense sector, from traditional acquisition programs to open innovation contests. Szajnfarber received her bachelor's degree in Engineering Science from the University of Toronto and conducted her graduate work at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. There she earned dual masters' degrees in Aeronautics & Astronautics and Technology Policy and a doctorate in Engineering Systems. Outside of academia, Dr. Szajnfarber has worked as a systems engineer at MDA Space Missions (Canadarm Program) and Dynacon Inc. (Microsatellites); and as a researcher at the European Space Agency (Advanced Concepts Team) and NASA (studying multiple centers).

item.title: Zili Huang
item.description:
Zili Huang

PhD student, Population Health Sciences (Health Systems Track)
email zilihuang@fas.harvard.edu

Zili Huang is a PhD student in the Population Health Sciences Program, Health Systems Track. Her research focuses on healthcare management and organizational behavior, with the goal of improving the efficiency and effectiveness of health systems through an organizational perspective. She is particularly interested in how healthcare organizations adapt to policy changes, financial incentives, and market dynamics, as well as how these factors influence provider behavior and health outcomes. Zili holds a Bachelor of Management Sciences in Public Administration from Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China and a Master of Science in Global Health and Population from the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health.

item.title: Zifu Wang
item.description:
Zifu Wang

Postdoctoral Research Fellow, Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA)
email zifu_wang@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Google Scholar

Zifu Wang, PhD is a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA), whose research lies at the intersection of Geographic Information Science (GIS), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and computational science. His work focuses on transforming complex spatial and textual data into evidence-based intelligence for public-policy applications in humanitarian response, crime prevention, public health, and transportation safety.

item.title: Zichao Li
item.description:
Zichao Li

PhD candidate in Population Health Sciences, Chan School of Public Health
email zichaoli@g.harvard.edu

Zichao Li is a PhD candidate in Population Health Sciences at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Her research focuses on how health communication, especially health misinformation, influences vaccine and cancer-related health outcomes. She uses multilevel modeling and computational methods, including machine learning, to uncover social patterns in health behavior and disparities. Her work bridges public health, behavioral science, and data-driven analysis to inform health interventions.

item.title: Zhongyu Wei
item.description:
Zhongyu Wei

PhD Student in Human Development, Learning and Teaching, HGSE
email zhongyu_wei@g.harvard.edu

Zhongyu Wei is a PhD student in the Human Development, Learning and Teaching program at Harvard University. She is interested in exploring how young children's curiosity and inquiry drive their language and cognitive development. She uses quantitative and computational methods to analyze observational data. Zhongyu holds a B.A. in linguistics and economics from Tsinghua University and an EdM in international education policy from the Harvard Graduate School of Education.

Zhichao is a professor in the School of Mathematics at Sun Yat-sen University. His main interests are causal inference methodologies and contaminated data problems with applications in biomedical sciences and social sciences.

item.title: Zheng Ma
item.description:
Zheng Ma

PhD Student in Social Policy and Government, HKS
email zma@g.harvard.edu
Zheng Ma is a PhD student in Social Policy and Government at Harvard University. His research interests lie primarily in leveraging computational methods to understand social phenomena and solve social problems. He hope to explore more how data-driven approaches can reveal intricate social dynamics and inform policy decisions in various contexts. Prior to coming to Harvard, he obtained an M.A. in Social Sciences from the University of Chicago and a B.A. in Economics and Political Science from the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

item.description:
Zehua Li

PhD Student in Government and Social Policy, HKS
JD Candidate, Stanford Law School
email zli@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Zehua’s research explores the intersection of law, AI, and local politics. He uses computational methods to identify policy interventions that foster grassroots civic participation and strengthen institutional capacity.

item.title: Zachary Lim
item.description:
Zachary Lim

PhD Candidate, Education
email zlim@g.harvard.edu
Zac investigates how researchers navigate methodological disputes by employing natural language processing techniques to analyze the meaning of contentious words in academic publications. He's also interested in using this approach to augment philosophical analyses of concepts.

item.title: Zach Wehrwein
item.description:
Zach Wehrwein

Sociology, GSAS
email wehrwein@fas.harvard.edu
My research interests are in social network analysis, causal inference, political sociology, and ecoNomic history. With IQSS support, I am currently working with a network scientist to create a very large dataset of social media usage during the 2016 election. I am also currently working on two smaller scale social network analysis projects, one concerning financial markets in the 17th century and aNother examining friendships in American high schools in the 1990s.

item.title: Yuyang Shi
item.description:
Yuyang Shi

IQSS Computer Lab Assistant
email yuyangshi@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Yuyang Shi's research centers around authoritarian politics and state-society relations. Specifically, his current project explores the concept of digital authoritarian resilience, examining how authoritarian regimes bolster their online propaganda efforts in the multimedia age.

item.title: Yujing Huang
item.description:
Yujing Huang

Department of Linguistics, GSAS
person
email yujinghuang@fas.harvard.edu
Yujing Huang is a doctoral student in the Department of Linguistics. She is interested in how different components of language interact. Her research interest includes how meaning is expressed via syntactic structure (that is, the syntax-semantics interface) and how sound structure interacts with this structure as well (i.e., the phoNology-syntax interface). Her approach to these questions is mainly experimental. She is currently working on an eye-tracking experiment with adults on the interpretation of verbal arguments. She is also working on a tonal dialect in China.

  • People By Last Name

    H - J

  • Role

    Affiliated Graduate Students

Original source: https://iq.harvard.edu/people/yujing-huang

item.title: Yuhua Wang
item.description:
Yuhua Wang

Associate Professor of Government
email yuhuawang@fas.harvard.edu

I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Government at Harvard University. My research has focused on state institutions and state-business relations in China. I am the author of Tying the Autocrat’s Hands: The Rise of the Rule of Law in China (Cambridge University Press, 2015).

item.title: Yuhao Wang
item.description:
Yuhao Wang

MA Candidate, Regional Studies East Asia Program, GSAS
email yuhaowang@fas.harvard.edu

Yuhao Wang is an MA candidate in the Regional Studies East Asia (RSEA) Program. He received his BA in Philosophy, Politics & Economics (PPE) from Renmin University. His research uses quantitative tools as well as data science techniques to analysis the interaction of China’s foreign and domestic politics as well as broader concerns in the international political economy. His recent study has been presented at the Asian Political Methodology Meeting, the MPSA, and the APSA annual meeting.

item.title: Yuhan Zhang
item.description:
Yuhan Zhang

PhD Student in Linguistics, GSAS
email yuz551@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Yuhan Zhang holds a BA in English Language and Literature from Tsinghua University, China, and is currently a doctoral student in the Department of Linguistics at Harvard, working with Kathryn Davidson. She is interested in using experimental and quantitative methods to understand semantics and pragmatics during communication and information interpretation. She also works on adult language processing by using the self-paced reading method to unravel the cognitive mechanisms underlying language comprehension and production. Her current experimental semantic project involves identifying contextual factors in conversations that cause referential opacity in belief reports. She is also using corpus linguistic methodologies to investigate the phonological correlation between stress shift and vowel reduction in English verb nominalization.

item.title: Yuan Pei
item.description:
Yuan Pei

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, GSAS
email yuanpei@g.harvard.edu

Yuan Pei is a doctoral student in Public Policy at Harvard. Yuan's research focuses on development economics and behavioral economics. His current research explores how changing social norms can affect people's decision to enter the civil service. Another project of his is studying the impact of distance learning on reducing urban-rural education gap in China. Prior to beginning his PhD, Yuan worked for two years at the University of Pennsylvania and received his BA from UC Berkeley.

item.description:
Yousra Neberai

PhD Candidate, Public Policy (Economics), GSAS
email yousraneberai@g.harvard.edu

Yousra is a PhD Candidate in the Public Policy program (Economics Track). She studies economic development, particularly related to the provision of public goods, infrastructure, and health. Prior to her PhD, she worked as a pre-doctoral research fellow at Yale and at NERA economic consulting. She graduated from Harvard College where she studied Government.

Takemi Fellow, Chan School of Public Health
Associate Professor at Kyoto University
email nishikawa.yoshitaka.4x@kyoto-u.ac.jp
laptop_windows Website
Yoshitaka Nishikawa is a physician-scientist with MD (Clinical Oncology and Gastroenterology) and PhD (Medicine). His research interests span clinical research, disaster epidemiology, and case study methodology. He employs quantitative methods, including spatial epidemiology, time series analysis, and statistical approach for studying rare events.

item.title: Yoonje (Jea) Lee
item.description:
Yoonje (Jea) Lee

PhD Student in Government, GSAS
email yoonjelee@g.harvard.edu

Yoonje (Jea) is a PhD Student in the department of Government at Harvard University. She is broadly interested in comparative political behavior, political economy of development, intergroup relations and migration in the Global South, particularly Southeast Asia. Methodologically, she is interested in causal inference and unstructured data.

item.title: Yoon Jin Lee
item.description:
Yoon Jin Lee

Government, GSAS
email yjlee@fas.harvard.edu
I am a PhD candidate in the Government Department focusing on international relations/foreign policy. My dissertation explores the British and South Korean legislators' dispositional and situational determinants of intuitive vs. deliberative foreign policy decision-making, specifically with regards to the UK/ROK's bilateral security relations with the United States. I use a variety of methods, including inductive discourse analysis, automated text analysis, and experiments.

item.title: Yoly Yue Liu
item.description:
Yoly Yue Liu

PhD Student in Education Policy, GSAS/HGSE
email yolyliu@g.harvard.edu

Yoly Yue Liu is a PhD student studying how education drives systemic change. Her research uses causal inference to identify effective education policies and practices in low- and middle-income countries. Through field experiments, she investigates how teaching practices can cultivate intrinsically motivated, interactive, and lasting learning; how communities can mobilize local resources to support learning and address local challenges; and how stakeholders can collaborate to foster a learning culture rooted in local realities. Before her PhD, Yoly worked as a policy researcher with United Nations agencies.

item.title: Yizhou Jin
item.description:
Yizhou Jin

GSAS + HBS, Economics
email yizhoujin@fas.harvard.edu
Yizhou is a PhD student in the Business Economics program at GSAS and HBS. His research interests span topics in labor ecoNomics, development ecoNomics and corporate finance. Yizhou holds a BA degree in Mathematics and Economics at UC Berkeley. He also worked for Citigroup, Houlihan Lokey, and SiNochem Petroleum before coming to Harvard.

item.title: Yinan Wang
item.description:
Yinan Wang

PhD Candidate in Sociology, GSAS
email yinanwang@g.harvard.edu

Yinan is a doctoral student in Sociology at Harvard University. Her research interests broadly focus on exploring how social institutions interact to create inequalities and stratifications along axes of gender, class, and race in the labor market using experiment methods. She received her BA in English and Sociology from Tsinghua University.

item.title: Yichen Guan
item.description:
Yichen Guan

Graduate School of Arts and Science, Harvard University, Department of Middle Eastern Studies
email yichenguan@fas.harvar.edu
Yichen Guan is a second year master student in Middle Eastern Studies. Her current research mainly focuses on the relationship between the diverse forms of authoritarianism and authoritarian persistence in the Middle East. She is interested in quantitative methodology in political science, and she is currently working as a research assistant for Professor Gary King and Molly Roberts. In her future research, she plans to combine the rigorous theory of comparative authoritarianism in the Middle East with sound empirical testing with programming skills such as R and STATA.

item.title: Yenee Soh
item.description:
Yenee Soh

Research Associate, Social and Behavioral Sciences, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
email ysoh@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows HSPH Profile

 

Yenee Soh, ScD, SM, is a social epidemiologist and research associate in the Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Dr. Soh earned her doctorate in Social and Behavioral Sciences and master’s in Epidemiology from the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. She also holds a bachelor’s degree in Mathematics from Macalester College. Previously, she was a post-doctoral research fellow at the Kaiser Permanente Northern California Division of Research.

item.title: Yaron Singer
item.description:
Yaron Singer

Associate in Computer Science
email yaron@seas.harvard.edu

Yaron Singer is an Associate in Computer Science. He was previously Gordon McKay Professor of Computer Science and Applied Mathematics at Harvard; prior to that he was a postdoctoral researcher at Google Research and obtained his PhD from UC Berkeley. He is the recipient of the 2012 Best Student Paper Award at the ACM conference on Web Search and Data Mining, the 2010 Facebook Fellowship, the 2009 Microsoft Research Fellowship, and several entrepreneurial awards for work on social networks.

item.title: Xihong Lin
item.description:
Xihong Lin

Coordinating Director of the Program in Quantitative Genomics
Professor of Biostatistics, TH Chan School of Public Health
Professor of Statistics, Faculty of Arts and Sciences
smartphone 617-432-2914
email xlin@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows HSPH profile
laptop_windows Research group website

Dr. Lin’s research interests lie in statistical genetics and genomics. She is interested in development and application of statistical and computational methods for analysis of high-throughput genetic, genomic and ‘omics data in epidemiological, environmental and clinical sciences,  especially genetic and epigenetic epidemiology, genes and environment, and medical genomics. Current research projects include genome-wide array association studies, whole genome sequencing association studies, gene-environment interactions, and genome-wide DNA methylation studies, pathway and network analysis, and integrative genetics and genomics. She currently serves as the coordinating director of the Program of Quantitative Genomics of Harvard School of Public Heath http://www.hsph.harvard.edu/pqg.  Her methodological work is currently supported by the MERIT award from the National Cancer Institute on “Statistical Methods for Correlated and High-Dimensional Biomedical Data,” and the PO1  from the National Cancer Institute on “Statistical Informatics in Cancer Research.” She is the contacting PI for the training grant  entitled Joint training in biostatistics and computational biology.

Associate Professor of Sociology

Xiang Zhou is an Associate Professor of Sociology at Harvard University. His research focuses on quantitative methodology, causes and consequences of economic inequality, and political culture in contemporary China. He has published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Social Forces, Sociological Methodology, Sociological Research and Methods, and China Economic Review. item.title: Wei Luo
item.description:
Wei Luo

Associate, Center for Geographic Analysis
Research Fellow in Pediatrics, Harvard Medical School
email wei.luo@childrens.harvard.edu

Dr. Wei Luo is a Research Associate at the Computational Health Informatics Program (CHIP) at Boston Children's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, and an Associate at the Center for Geographic Analysis at Harvard University. He has broad training backgrounds in Geography (GIScience), Public Health (Epidemiology), Computer Science (Visual Analytics), and Network Science. He proposed a new research area: geo-social visual analytics, which aims to integrate spatial analysis, social network analysis, and machine learning into visual analytics environments. He has designed and developed a series of geovisual analytics tools and high performance computational models for infectious disease transmission and control (i.e., influenza, HIV), international trade, social media, crime analysis, climate change impacts, and water crisis.

item.title: Wei
item.description:
Wei-Fang Hsieh

PhD Student in Linguistics, GSAS
email weifanghsieh@g.harvard.edu

Wei-Fang is a third year PhD student in the Department of Linguistics at Harvard. Her main research areas are syntax, semantics, the syntax-semantics interface and laboratory phonology. Her research interests include the syntax and semantics of reduplication, ellipsis and resultatives, and the phonetics and phonology of tone sandhi. Wei-Fang uses experimental methodology to investigate the tone sandhi pattern in Mandarin-English code-switching context. She seeks to relate the empirical data collected using experimental methods to the phonological theory. In particular, her current phonology project investigates how the phonotactic differences between Mandarin and English may have a distance effect on the application of tone sandhi in speech production of Mandarin native speakers code-switching between the two languages. Before coming to Harvard, Wei-Fang received her M.A. in Linguistics and B.A. in Foreign Languages and Literatures from National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan.

item.description:
Vinay Mehra

Associate of IQSS
email vmehra@iq.harvard.edu

Vinay Mehra is passionate about curating academic literature to make new knowledge accessible to a broader audience, highlighting the crucial role of social science and data in shaping our understanding of the world. His research focuses on identifying and nurturing social science ideas that can be developed into commercial ventures for public benefit. With a strong background in the media and information sectors, Vinay has held leadership roles at Politico and The Boston Globe and has successfully launched multiple companies as an entrepreneur in the data and information space.

item.title: Victor Yang
item.description:
Victor Yang

Computer Science, Harvard College
email vyang@college.harvard.edu
Victor Yang is a sophomore from Boston, MA living in Leverett House. He is concentrating in Computer Science with a secondary in Psychology. He is working on the project "Harnessing machine learning to understand change in social attitudes" with PhD students Benedek Kurdi and Tessa Charlesworth in Professor Mahzarin R. Banaji's lab. The project attempts to use word embedding data from a large set of writings to analyze how people's biases have changed over time and how these biases develop within children. Victor is interested in conducting research at the intersection of computer science and other fields. Outside of research, he is a co-president of Harvard's Engineers Without Borders chapter.

He carries out research on educational programs that promote community-based research, investigation, and dialogue with the aim of advancing community development. His current projects leverage randomized controlled trials and other tools of causal inference to study the impacts of such programs in Uganda and Colombia.

item.title: Valerie O'Kane
item.description:
Valerie O'Kane

Director of Business Operations
email valerie_okane@harvard.edu
  • Linkedin

Valerie O’Kane is the Director of Business Operations at the Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) at Harvard University with responsibility for the IQSS budget & long term planning, IQSS contract and grant administration, IQSS compliance & space, as well as managing the general day-to-day operations of the IQSS Business Office.

Valerie is a seasoned financial professional who began her career at Harvard in 1992 as a staff assistant in the Central Payroll Office. In 1994 she moved to the FAS Extension School as the Payroll Administrator/Assistant Human Resource Officer. Valerie returned to Harvard Central Administration in 2002 as an accounting assistant and in 2004 became one of two payroll accountants for the University. Valerie was recognized as a Harvard Hero in 2005 as part of the University’s W2 Team. In 2007 she moved to the Harvard Kennedy School as the Financial Manager of the Malcolm Wiener Center for Social Policy. Valerie joined the Harvard College Observatory in 2018 as the Assistant Director of Finance and Administration. Valerie stepped in as the Interim Executive Director of the HCO for 1 year (2021-2022) and in 2023 was promoted to the Associate Director of Finance and Administration & Chief Financial Officer.

Valerie has received her Masters in Business Management from Harvard - Division of Continuing as well as two Harvard Graduate Certificates in Strategic Management & Business Communication. She received her Bachelors of Science and Business Administration from Suffolk University and her Associate Degree from Bay State College - Boston.

item.title: Uma M Ilavarasan
item.description:
Uma M Ilavarasan

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email uilavarasan@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Uma Ilavarasan is a graduate student in the Department of Government at Harvard University, where she studies American and comparative politics, with a focus on American political development, comparative political economy, bureaucratic politics, and social and industrial policy. She employs both qualitative (interview and archival work) and quantitative methods in her work.

item.title: Tyler VanderWeele
item.description:
Tyler VanderWeele

John L. Loeb and Frances Lehman Loeb Professor of Epidemiology, Harvard School of Public Health
Director of the Human Flourishing Program
email tvanderw@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Profile (Human Flourishing Program)

Tyler J. VanderWeele is the John L. Loeb and Frances Lehman Loeb Professor of Epidemiology in the Departments of Epidemiology and Biostatistics at the Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health, and director of the Human Flourishing Program and co-director of the Initiative on Health, Religion and Spirituality at Harvard University. He holds degrees from the University of Oxford, University of Pennsylvania, and Harvard University in mathematics, philosophy, theology, finance, and biostatistics. His methodological research is focused on theory and methods for distinguishing between association and causation in the biomedical and social sciences and, more recently, on psychosocial measurement theory. His empirical research spans psychiatric and social epidemiology; the science of happiness and flourishing; and the study of religion and health. He is the recipient of the 2017 Presidents’ Award from the Committee of Presidents of Statistical Societies (COPSS). He has published over three hundred papers in peer-reviewed journals; is author of the books Explanation in Causal Inference (2015), Modern Epidemiology (2021), and Measuring Well-Being (2021); and he also writes a monthly blog posting on topics related to human flourishing for Psychology Today.

item.title: Torben Iversen
item.description:
Torben Iversen

Harold Hitchings Burbank Professor of Political Economy, Department of Government
location_on CGIS Knafel K308
email iversen@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Torben Iversen is Harold Hitchings Burbank Professor of Political Economy in the Government Department at Harvard University. His research and teaching interests include comparative political economy, electoral politics, and applied formal theory. He is the author or co-author of Big Data and the Welfare State: How the Information Revolution Threatens Social Solidarity (Cambridge UP 2022) (with Philipp Rehm), Democracy and Prosperity: Reinventing Capitalism through a Turbulent Century (Princeton UP 2019) (with David Soskice), Women, Work, and Politics: The Political Economy of Gender Inequality (Yale UP 2010) (with Frances Rosenbluth), Capitalism, Democracy, and Welfare (Cambridge UP 2005), and Contested Economic Institutions (Cambridge UP 1999). He is also the author or co-author of more than four dozen articles on comparative politics and political economy. He is a member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences and has been a Radcliffe Fellow, a Guggenheim Fellow, a Hoover National Fellow, and a BP Centennial Professor at the London School of Economics.

item.title: Thierry Warin
item.description:
Thierry Warin

Visiting Scholar, Digital Data Design (D^3) Institute
email thierry.warin@mail.analytics.hbs.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

A Visiting Scholar at the Digital Data Design (D^3) Institute, Thierry Warin is an academic whose professional pursuits are deeply rooted in the application of data science methodologies to explore global transformations, including phenomena such as the platform economy and the dynamics of innovation. His research is characterized by the examination of institutions, economic dynamics, and the utilization of unstructured data to uncover insights into these complex areas. 
Warin places a high value on democratizing access to the burgeoning field of data science, viewing it as a pivotal step toward collaboratively addressing global challenges. His approach to research is fundamentally inductive, prioritizing a bottom-up methodology that is deeply rooted in data. This perspective not only enriches empirical understanding across various domains but also serves as a robust framework for testing, refining, and potentially redefining theoretical constructs and traditional economic indicators.

item.title: Théo Voldoire
item.description:
Théo Voldoire

PhD Candidate, Statistics
email theovoldoire@fas.harvard.edu
Théo is a first year Ph.D. student at the Harvard Statistics Department. Trained both as a mathematician and social scientist, he specializes in computational methods for ecological inference, sequence and text analysis, and causal inference. He has studied the evolution of revolving doors patterns among French elite civil servants since the 1990s, as well as the causal relationship between company mergers and employment.

item.description:
Taeyoung Kim

Harvard College, Leverett House
Department of Government, Secondary field in French Language & Literature
email taeyoungkim14@college.harvard.edu
Having lived extensively in 7 different cities in 6 different countries that she now affectionately calls her "home," she is interested in the impact of globalization on human mobility, international trade, international development, transnational identities and human rights. At IQSS, she works with Professor Beth Simmons and she codes official border presence along international corridors. She is interested in learning and researching more about the different immigration policies and the rationales, effects, and amendments, from a comparative perspective. Outside of the classroom, she spends her time volunteering at PBHA, exploring the city (museums, plays, movies, historical sites), drawing and reading.

item.title: SV Subramanian
item.description:
SV Subramanian

Professor of Population Health and Geography, Harvard School of Public Health
smartphone p: 617-432-6299
email svsubram@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

S ("Subu") V Subramanian is a Professor of Population Health and Geography at the Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences, Harvard Chan School of Public Health, and a senior core faculty at the Harvard Center for Population and Development Studies. He is also the founding director for an interdisciplinary PhD program in Population Health Sciences that is currently being developed at Harvard. He received his under- and post-graduate training at the University of Delhi, and completed his PhD in geography from the University of Portsmouth, UK in 2000. Subu has published over 425 articles, book chapters, and books in the field of social and contextual determinants of health, health inequalities in India with a special emphasis on nutritional inequalities among children and adults, and applied multilevel statistical models. He has co-edited 2 books titled Global Perspectives on Social Capital and Health and Social Capital and Health. Subu was the first to develop a course on the concept and application of multilevel statistical methods at Harvard, which he has been successfully teaching at Harvard Chan since 2001, as well as around the world. He has advised over 100 masters, doctoral and postdoctoral students as mentor, academic advisor and dissertation committee member. Subu has served as a permanent member of the National Institutes of Health Community Influences on Health Behavior (CIHB) Study Section, and as reviewer for several national and international funding agencies. He is the Editor-in-Chief (along with Ichiro Kawachi) for the international journal Social Science & Medicine (SSM), in addition to be being a Senior Editor for the social epidemiology office of SSM. He is an editorial consultant to The Lancet, an international advisory board member for theLancet Global Health, and serves as an academic editor to PLoS Medicine, besides serving as an editorial board member of several journals.

item.title: Sung In Kim
item.description:
Sung In Kim

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email sunginkim@g.harvard.edu

Sung In Kim is a PhD student in the Department of Government at Harvard University. She studies comparative political economy, international political economy, and political behavior. Her research focuses on how structural changes in economy affect political attitudes toward immigration and redistribution as well as voting behavior.

item.title: Sun Young Park
item.description:
Sun Young Park

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email sunyoungpark@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Sun Young Park is a PhD candidate in the Department of Government at Harvard University. She studies political behavior and electoral politics in advanced democracies. Her research addresses how political divisions and animosities between social groups are shaped by the interplay of economic structure, social environments, and psychological mechanisms. To theorize the interactions of these macro-, meso-, and micro-level factors, she combines perspectives of political economy, political sociology, and political psychology. Specifically, her work has focused on why gender and geographic divides in political attitudes arise in contemporary democracies. She uses big data analysis, deep learning, spatial analysis, statistical causal inference, and survey methodology to answer these questions.

item.title: Stephen Kraffmiller
item.description:
Stephen Kraffmiller

Software Developer, Dataverse Project
email skraffmiller@hmdc.harvard.edu

Stephen has been a devloper on the Dataverse Project since 2010. He is involved in all aspects of the Dataverse project, from the front end UI development to the back end business logic. For the Dataverse project he was the lead developer for custom dataset metadata fields, persistent identifiers, guestbook and download tracking.  He also implemented dataset templates and external query tools. He has given three presentations at JavaOne conferences over the years including "jQuery Integration with JSF 2 Libraries" with Mike Heppler and "Custom Bean Validation" with Gustavo Durand.  Currently he is spearheading the effort to make Dataverse data available via the Google Data Commons. In his spare time he enjoys running, reading, cooking and following the career of a talented playwright who happens to be his daughter.

item.title: Ismail Erkam Sula
item.description:
Ismail Erkam Sula

Post-Doctoral Researcher, IQSS
Assistant Professor of International Relations, Ankara Yıldırım Beyazıt University
email sula@bilkent.edu.tr
laptop_windows Papers
laptop_windows Twitter
Dr. Sula specializes in the study of foreign policy and research methodology in social sciences. He is currently working on the application of computer assisted methods in data collection. His research combines event data analysis with content analysis to observe the parallelism of words and deeds in foreign policy. As part of his PhD research at Bilkent University, Erkam collected data about Turkey's foreign policy words and deeds. As a result of this data collection process he developed the Turkish Foreign Policy Roles and Events Dataset (TFPRED) which contains data gathered from Turkish leaders' speeches and 36.000 International news reports. He has previously published on Role Theory, Event Data, Middle East, Turkish foreign policy and eclectic methodology in foreign policy analysis.  Dr. Sula is a faculty member at Ankara Yildirim Beyazit University, Faculty of Political Science. He is also a member of International Studies Association (ISA), American Political Science Association (APSA), and European International Studies Association (EISA).

Siddharth George is a PhD student at the Department of EcoNomics and the Harvard Kennedy School. His research interests are in development ecoNomics, political ecoNomy and labour ecoNomics. He is also currently writing a paper on the long-term impact of the Partition of India, probably the largest population movement of the 20th century.

item.description:
Shira Zilberstein

PhD Candidate in Sociology, GSAS
Science and Technology Studies Fellow, Harvard Kennedy School
email szilberstein@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Shira Zilberstein is a PhD candidate in sociology at Harvard University and a fellow in the Science and Technology Studies program. Her focuses on cultural sociology, science and technology studies and organizations, as well as theory and methods. She is interested in the production, interpretation and evaluation of ideas and the dynamics between hegemonic and counter-hegemonic forms of knowledge in institutional and technical settings. She focuses on qualitative and computational methods. Her dissertation focuses on applied interdisciplinary research collaborations and the ways in which knowledge is put into practice to define and address social problems.

item.description:
Sharon-Lise Normand

S. James Adelstein Professor of Health Care Policy, Harvard Medical School
Professor in the Department of Biostatistics, TH Chan School of Public Health
smartphone 617-432-3260
email normand@hcp.med.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Sharon-Lise T. Normand, PhD, is the S. James Adelstein Professor of Health Care Policy at Harvard Medical School and Professorin the Department of Biostatistics at the Harvard School of Public Health. Dr. Normand’s research focuses on the development of statistical methods for health services research, primarily using Bayesian approaches to problem solving, including assessment of quality of care, methods for causal inference, provider profiling, meta-analysis, and latent variable modeling. She has developed a long line of research on methods for the analysis of patterns of treatment and quality of care for patients with cardiovascular disease and with mental disorders in particular.

item.title: Shannon Lynn Parker
item.description:
Shannon Lynn Parker

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email shannonparker@g.harvard.edu

Shannon is a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Government. Her research focuses on violence against women (particularly women of color), state government, local civil courts, and political attitudes and behaviors. Her dissertation grounds these issues in the United States justice system, while smaller research projects focus on how privacy is defined in this and other contexts in China. Prior to arriving at Harvard, she was a Research Associate in the Liberty & National Security Program at the Brennan Center for Justice at NYU School of Law. She also lived in Beijing as a Princeton in Asia fellow and conducted research in Qingdao as a fellow in the Fulbright-Hays Group Program Abroad. She graduated from Brown University with an A.B. in Public Policy & American Institutions and East Asian Studies.

item.title: Seth Soderborg
item.description:
Seth Soderborg

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email soderborg@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

I am a PhD student in government with research in the world's third- and fourth-largest democracies—Indonesia and Brazil. The goal of my research is to understand how developing country governments conduct public administration under hard research constraints, identify consistent biases in the procedures used to collect government data in these countries, and, separately, to build comprehensive databases of historical election results. With Daniel Ziblatt, I have worked on using local election data from 1930s Germany to study party politics. With Horacio Larreguy and Stephen Ansolabehere advising, I have run my own work on collecting precinct-level election data from printed archives, carrying out election forensics analysis of those results to study election fraud, and making data available in usable forms for researchers and the public in Brazil and Indonesia.

item.title: Seth Rosenberg
item.description:
Seth Rosenberg

Undergraduate Student, Harvard College
email sethrosenberg@college.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Seth Rosenberg studies power. Specifically, He works to conceptually clarify and measure power in international relations (IR). What is power, and how can we talk about it? Who holds power, and how does it manifest in individuals, groups, nations, and other actors? Which methods of exercising power are most effective? These questions have endlessly fascinated Rosenberg as an instrument and researcher of IR.

item.title: Serhat Yildirim
item.description:
Dr. Serhat Yildirim

Serhat Yildirim, MD, graduated from Ghent University Medical School in Belgium. Currently enrolled in the MMSc Global Health Delivery program at Harvard Medical School, he holds the prestigious Fulbright and Fayat Scholarships. Driven by a passion for global and social health, his work primarily centers on (mental) healthcare and a keen focus on the availability and accessibility of (mental) healthcare services.

Selim Yaman is a PhD candidate in Political Science at American University's School of Public Affairs, where he specializes in political methodology and comparative politics. His research focuses on natural language processing and Bayesian statistics. Yaman also serves as a Fellow at the Center for Data Science at American University and replication analyst at Political Analysis. Before starting doctoral study, he lived and worked in the UK, and Turkey.

item.title: Sebastian Muñoz
item.description:
Sebastian Muñoz-Najar Galvez

Bluhm Family Assistant Professor of Data Science and Education
email sebastian_munozng@gse.harvard.edu

Sebastian Munoz-Najar Galvez is the Bluhm Family Assistant Professor of Data Science and Education at the Harvard Graduate School of Education. Sebastian received a doctoral degree in Sociology of Education from the Graduate School of Education at Stanford University. He is a sociologist of science who uses computational linguistics and network analysis to study the ebb and flow of research agendas. He has recently turned toward the study of adolescent experience, college admissions, and technologies of belonging. Sebastian's work has been published in the American Sociological Review, the Proceedings of the National Academy of Science, and American Educational Research Journal.

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS

Sarah Mohamed in a PhD Candidate in Government. She studies comparative politics and international relations with a focus on the Middle East and North Africa. Specifically, she is interested in political communication and public opinion. Methodologically, her research employs text analysis and survey experiments. Prior to coming to Harvard, Sarah worked in Washington D.C. at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, earned her MPhil in Modern Middle Eastern Studies at St. Antony’s College, Oxford, and her BA in Political Science and Comparative Literature at the University of California, Berkeley.

item.title: Sarah Farnsworth Hatch
item.description:
Sarah Farnsworth Hatch

PhD Student in Education, Graduate School of Education
email shatch@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Sarah Farnsworth Hatch is a doctoral student studying prevention science and implementation science at the Harvard Graduate School of Education. She focuses on evaluating programs and practices which support children's social and emotional development, such as parenting interventions and school-based social and emotional learning (SEL). She also studies implementation barriers to these programs, and her current work is centered around politicized opposition to SEL and parents' censorship of schooling materials. She holds an MSEd in Teaching, Learning, and Leadership from the University of Pennsylvania and a BS in Sociology from Brigham Young University.

item.title: Sara Ji
item.description:
Sara Ji

PhD Student in Education Policy, GSAS
email saraji@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Sara is a first year PhD student and a presidential scholar at Harvard studying education policy and the economics of education. Sara’s main research interests lie in applied econometrics, the economics of education, and labor economics, with a focus on inequality. She is passionate about using quantitative methods to understand and improve inequality in education and the labor market. Prior to graduate studies, Sara worked as a Pre-Doctoral research fellow at the National Bureau of Economic Research on projects related to the impact of a desegregation busing program on student outcomes. She also worked as a Research Associate at Harvard Business School. Sara graduated from Brandeis University in 2021 with a BS in applied math, a BA in economics and a minor in philosophy.

Through IQSS, I have had the opportunity to quantitatively analyze the plight of German workers in response to their local business tax, as well as the determinants and tax-induced effects of on foreign investment.

Salil Vadhan is the Vicky Joseph Professor of Computer Science and Applied Mathematics in the Harvard University School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, and the Director of the Harvard Center for Research on Computation and Society. He received his PhD in Applied Mathematics from MIT in 1999, and was an NSF Postdoctoral Fellow at MIT and the Institute for Advanced Study before joining the Harvard faculty in 2001. He is a recipient of a Simons Investigator Award, a Godel Prize, a Guggenheim Fellowship, a Phi Beta Kappa Award for Excellence in Teaching, and the ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award.

item.title: Sakurako S Okuzono
item.description:
Sakurako S Okuzono

PhD Candidate in Population Health Sciences, Department of Social Behavioral Sciences, Chan School of Public Health
email sshiba@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Google

Sakurako is a PhD candidate in the Population Health Sciences Program at the Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health. Her research focuses on the following areas: 1) understanding the social determinants of children's health and well-being, 2) examining the impact of natural disasters, and 3) applying computational methods, including machine learning algorithms, to the measurement of social phenomena. This includes exploring the complex, interconnected nature of composite indices, using remote sensing data to measure neighborhood trends over time, and employing a stochastic intervention framework.

item.title: Ryan Olson
item.description:
Ryan S Olson

Faculty Affiliate, Human Flourishing Program
email ryan@aret.ai
laptop_windows Website
  • Twitter
  • Linkedin

Ryan S. Olson is a research professor at the University of Virginia and a senior fellow at the Institute for Advanced Studies in Culture. A classicist by training, Olson’s current scholarship focuses on the literary, historical, religious, and cultural context of ancient education from Archaic Greece through Late Antiquity, offering comparative insights for periods of cultural transformation, including the late modern world.

Olson is co-director of the program on culture and formation at the Institute for Advanced Studies in Culture at the University of Virginia. He is the founder of the Moral Ecology Trust, a network of senior leaders from various sectors of American society who gather to pool their expertise, networks, and influence to improve the moral ecosystems that form students, citizens, and leaders.

Olson earned graduate degrees in theology, Asian and Middle Eastern studies, and classical languages and literature from Durham University and Oxford University, completing his D.Phil. in Classics at Oxford (Trinity College). His book, Tragedy, Authority, and Trickery: The Poetics of Embedded Letters in Josephus (Harvard University Press), is a study of narrative epistolography in its historical and cultural contexts. He is also co-editor of The Content of Their Character and of The Necessity of Character. He is writing a book on Aristotle’s moral ecology and the reception of the classical tradition in American education. His larger research agenda aims to understand the continuities and discontinuities among the diverse cultures of education across the ancient Mediterranean world, drawing on archaeological, literary, and documentary sources in Greek, Latin, and Syriac. His work also attempts to translate insights from antiquity to the contemporary world using micro- and macro-social theories of subjectivity, morality, education, and knowledge.

Previously, Olson directed the Institute for Advanced Studies in Culture at the University of Virginia and served as president of the Advanced Studies in Culture Foundation (2016–2024), where he was responsible for administration, board development, and fundraising. He was a program director at the Kern Family Foundation, leading grant-making initiatives in educational reform and character education, and earlier directed an education policy department at a state-based think tank. He has been invited to give presentations and lectures by the Philanthropy Roundtable, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, the Association of Education Finance and Policy, the State Policy Network, and the U.S. Department of Education.

Olson has held fellowships at Harvard University’s Center for Hellenic Studies in Washington, DC. He is a faculty affiliate of the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University.

item.title: Ryan Enos
item.description:
Ryan Enos

Professor, Department of Government
email renos@gov.harvard.edu
Ryan D. Enos is a Professor of Government and Faculty Associate in the Institute for Quantitative Social Science specializing in American Politics, Political Psychology, and Race and Ethnic Politics. He studies political behavior and intergroup attitudes through laboratory and field experiments and other methods. He directs the Working Group in Political Psychology, an interdisciplinary forum for research on the microfoundations of citizen and elite behavior, and the Harvard Digital Lab for the Social Sciences. His research has been published in the American Political Science Review, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, and the American Journal of Political Science, in addition to other outlets, and has been covered in major media outlets such as the New York Times and the Washington Post. He earned his AB in political science and history from U.C. Berkeley and his MA and PhD in political science from UCLA. Before entering academia, he was a teacher at Paul Robeson High School in Chicago, IL.

item.title: Roza Nalbandyan
item.description:
Roza Nalbandyan

Postdoctoral Fellow, Human Flourishing Program
email rozanalbandyan@fas.harvard.edu

Roza Nalbandyan is a Postdoctoral Fellow with the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University. Her research develops and applies advanced statistical and measurement models — including psychometrics, machine learning, and computational methods — to examine measurement comparability, validity, student development, and academic flourishing. She received her Ph.D. in Education (Measurement, Assessment, and Evaluation) and M.S. in Statistics from Stanford University, and an Ed.M. in Higher and Postsecondary Education from Columbia University, Teachers College. Her work has been recognized with multiple fellowships and awards, including the 2026 NCBE Validity & Fairness Research Award, the James and Viola Quillen Fellowship, and the AERA Outstanding Research Award. Before pursuing her doctorate, she spent over six years in university administration, an experience that continues to ground her research in the practical realities of education.

item.title: Roy Guyton
item.description:
Roy Guyton

IT Client Support Services Associate
email rguyton@hmdc.harvard.edu

Roy is a graduate of Willamette University where he received a BA in Spanish Literature and International Studies in Salem, OR. He also received his Ed.M for Technology in Education from the Harvard Graduate School of Education. Additionally, Roy has taken graduate courses at NIH, MIT and the Division of Continuing Education (DCE) at Harvard University. Roy was employed with the Harvard Graduate School of Education for 12 years where he ran the student help desk, and employed 10 years at DCE as a graduate teaching assistant. He spent three years in Costa Rica as a US Peace Corps volunteer working with IBM , MIT, Omar Dengo Foundation and the Costa Rican Department of Education on a national computer education program. He holds extensive contracting experience since 1992 in the area of technology in education. Roy was also a board member of several non profit organizations.

item.title: Rony Rodrigo Maximiliano Rodriguez
item.description:
Rony Rodrigo Maximiliano Rodriguez-Ramirez

PhD Candidate in Education Policy and Program Evaluation, HGSE
email rrodriguezramirez@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows GitHub

Rony Rodriguez is a PhD student in Education Policy and Program Evaluation at Harvard University whose work centers on understanding and mitigating the impacts of major disruptions, such as pandemics, conflict, and displacement, on educational outcomes and human capital development. His research investigates how targeted educational and mental health interventions can strengthen student well-being and resilience, reduce inequality, and improve long-term educational and economic trajectories for vulnerable populations. He focuses in particular on school-based interventions like individualized tutoring, enhanced curricula, and integrated mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS). He holds an M.D.P. in Development Policy from KDI School (South Korea) and a B.A. in Applied Economics from Universidad Centroamericana (Nicaragua).

item.description:
Roman Klimke

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, HKS and GSAS

Roman Klimke is a Ph.D. candidate in Public Policy at Harvard University. His research interests lie at the intersection of labor and public economics. His current research investigates how labor markets respond to the introduction of social programs, for which he uses individual- and establishment-level Social Security data from Germany and Saudi Arabia. Roman holds a B.Sc. degree in International Economics and Middle Eastern Studies from the University of Tuebingen and spent one year abroad at the University of Michigan during his undergraduate studies.

item.title: Roland Pongou
item.description:
Roland Pongou

Visiting Scientist, Department of Global Health and Population
Associate Professor of Economics, University of Ottawa
laptop_windows Website
Roland Pongou is a Visiting Scientist in the Department of Global Health and Population at Harvard and an Affiliate of the Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science. He is an Associate Professor of Economics at the University of Ottawa in Canada, and an affiliated faculty member of the African School of Economics in Benin. He received his PhD in Economics from Brown University. He also has graduate training in Mathematics and Demography. He was formerly a Fellow in the Takemi Program in International Health at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, a PDGA Scholar at Harvard, and a Visiting Scholar in the MIT Department of Economics. Professor Pongou's primary research interests are in political economy, health, economic development, and economic theory. His work focuses on the microeconomic analysis of formal and informal institutions, and on the effects of institutions on various aspects of individual and societal wellbeing. His recent research uses matching theory to study the effect of ethnic integration on sexual network formation and the spread of HIV and other sexually transmitted diseases in multiethnic societies. He has also published research that addresses questions in voting and political design, fairness in organizations, and gender inequalityn Professor Pongou's analytical approach combines microeconomic theory, game theory, social network theory, and quantitative methods. He has also published research that contributes to these areas. He is a recipient of multiple awards and fellowships. He is a member of the Econometric Society, the American Economic Association, the Canadian Economic Association, the African Finance and Economic Association, and the Population Association of America.

item.title: Robert Treacy
item.description:
Robert Treacy

Principal Software Architect and Data Engineer, Dataverse Project
email rtreacy@hmdc.harvard.edu

Bob Treacy is a Principal Software Architect and Data Engineer. He came to IQSS in 2006 at the beginning stages of the Dataverse Project and participated in the initial architecture, design and development of the system. He worked on a project for survey research, the Consilience project for text analysis, differential privacy, and maintained the Zelig project and various other R packages when they were hosted on CRAN. He has been working with Java since 1995, when he was at Fusion Systems Group, a consulting firm which worked closely with Sun Microsystems. He has over 35 years in software development for manufacturing, financial systems, education course management, news delivery and various web companies. In addition to Java, he has developed in Scala, C/C++, Python, and R. He has worked with various relational databases and NoSQL databases. His first distributed systems project in the early 90s involved integrating stove-piped legacy terminal-based manufacturing systems using modern UIs and in recent years has worked with Hadoop and Spark for machine learning and AI. He is currently involved with fine-tuning local Large Language Models using FAS RC on Dataverse data. He has worked with the AWS and Azure cloud platforms.

item.title: Robert Sampson
item.description:
Robert Sampson

Henry Ford II Professor of the Social Sciences, Department of Sociology
smartphone 617-496-9716
email rsampson@wjh.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
Robert J. Sampson is Henry Ford II Professor of the Social Sciences at Harvard University, where he was appointed in 2003. Before that he taught for twelve years in the Department of Sociology at the University of Chicago and for seven years at the University of IlliNois at Urbana-Champaign. Sampson was a Senior Research Fellow at the American Bar Foundation from 1994-2002, and in the 1997-98 and 2002-03 academic years he was a Fellow at the Center for Advanced Study in the Behavioral Sciences in Stanford, California. In 2005, he received a three-year RWJ Investigator Award in Health Policy Research and was inducted as a Fellow into the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.Professor Sampson's research interests center on crime and violence, the life course, neighborhood effects, and the sociology of the modern city. In the area of neighborhood effects and urban sociology his current work is focusing on the community-level context of well being, race/ethnicity and mechanisms of ecological inequality, the social meanings of disorder, the network structure of community influence, civic engagement, and other topics linked to community-level social processes. Much of this work stems from the Project on Human Development in Chicago Neighborhoods, for which Sampson serves as Scientific Director. He is also engaged in a longitudinal study from birth to death of 1,000 disadvantaged men born in Boston during the Great Depression era. Two publications from this project Crime in the Making: Pathways and Turning Points Through Life (Harvard, 1993) and Shared Beginnings, Divergent Lives: Delinquent Boys to Age 70 (Harvard, 2003), each received distinguished book awards from the American Society of CrimiNology, the Academy of Criminal Justice Sciences, and the Crime, Law, and Deviance Section of the American Sociological Association.

He studies these topics primarily through the use of quantitative methods on large administrative datasets gathered from governments, corporations, and the Internet. item.title: Robert Gaudin, MD
item.description:
Robert Gaudin, MD

Oral-Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery, Charité, Berlin
Visiting Fellow
email robert-andre.gaudin@charite.de

Doctor Gaudin graduated from Medical School, Hamburg, Germany and defended his doctoral thesis in 2014. From January 2015 until June 2016 he was a research fellow at Harvard Medical School, Department of Facial Plastics MGH/MEEI working on the development of a Bionic Face in an animal model. Besides that he was working on  basic clinical studies. In July 2016 he started working as a resident at the University Clinic, Hamburg, Eppendorf, Germany at the Department Oral- Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery. Besides his residency he has started studying dentistry at the University of Kiel in October 2016. Since then, it was of great interest for doctor Gaudin to find out how well doctors do on rating dental x-rays, since it is the number one diagnostic tool in the clinic. Also, if it is possible to create a superhuman algorithm diagnosing pathologies to improve the doctor's rating process. This is how he got involved with the Lab of Karim Lakhani at Harvard. Besides that he is doing Research on 3D printing of tissue. Since February 2017 doctor Gaudin switched his residency position to the Department Oral- Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery, Charite, Berlin and continued his projects from there.

item.title: Robert Gahl
item.description:
Robert Gahl

Visiting Faculty Fellow, Human Flourishing Program
Associate Professor of Ethics, Pontifical University of the Holy Cross
email robertgahl@gmail.com

Robert Gahl is the Vice Director of the Markets, Culture, and Ethics (MCE) Research Centre and of the Program of Church Management at the Pontifical University of the Holy Cross (Santa Croce) in Rome. Dr. Gahl completed his bachelor's degree in chemical engineering at Washington University in St. Louis, worked in Silicon Valley, completed his doctorate in philosophy at Santa Croce, and did postdoctoral research at the University of Notre Dame. Much of his recent research has focused on classical virtue theory, the narrative unity of life, and their complementarity with cognitive psychology. His current research focuses on moderation, self-mastery, and sexuality within human flourishing and is offering a course entitled "Self-Mastery, Sexuality, and Neuroscience: Perspectives from Philosophy and Cognitive Science for Human Flourishing": https://hfh.fas.harvard.edu/self-mastery

item.title: Richard Zeckhauser
item.description:
Richard Zeckhauser

Frank Plumpton Ramsey Professor of Political Economy, Harvard Kennedy School
smartphone 617-495-1174
email richard_zeckhauser@harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
Richard J. Zeckhauser is the Frank Plumpton Ramsey Professor of Political EcoNomy at Harvard University John F. Kennedy School of Government. Zeckhauser pursues a mix of conceptual and applied research. The primary challenge facing society, he believes, is to allocate resources in accordance with the preferences of the citizenry. Much of his conceptual work examines possibilities for democratic, decentralized, allocation procedures. His ongoing policy investigations explore ways to promote the health of human beings, to help labor and financial markets operate more efficiently, and to foster informed and appropriate choices by individuals and government agencies. Zeckhauser's current major research addresses the performance of institutions confronted with inadequate commitment capabilities, incomplete information flow and human participants who fail to behave in accordance with models of rationality (for example, by engaging in herd behavior). Financial markets and health risks are the subjects of his major empirical investigations.

item.title: Richard Wrangham
item.description:
Richard Wrangham

Ruth Moore Professor of Biological Anthropology, Emeritus, Department of Anthropology
smartphone 617-495-5948
email wrangham@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Richard Wrangham is the Ruth Moore Professor of Biological Anthropology, Emeritus. Professor Wrangham received a Ph.D. in Zoology from Cambridge University, where he studied under the renowned ethologist Robert Hinde. He served as a faculty member in departments of Anthropology, Psychology and Biology at several universities including Bristol University, Stanford University, King's College Cambridge and the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor.For several decades, Professor Wrangham has studied primates in the wild including several species of baboon and Vervet monkeys but his work on the ecological and behavior comparisons of chimpanzees and humans has been his greatest contribution to the animal behavior literature. His insights into the cultural similarities between humans and chimpanzees--including our unique tendencies to form murderous alliances and engage in recreational sexual activity--has had profound affects on how scientists analyze primate behavior, non-human and human alike. In addition to his exhaustive peer-reviewed journal publications, as author of Demonic Males: Apes and the Origins of Human Violence, Chimpanzee Cultures, and as co-editor of Primate Societies, Professor Wrangham's important observations and theoretical contributions to the field of primate socio-behavior are covered in a variety of works, which range from the textbook to popular science manual. In recent years, Professor Wrangham has been named as a trustee to several important primatological research organizations, including the Dian Fossey Gorilla Fund, the Jane Goodall Institute and is Chair of the Great Ape World Heritage Species Project. He is the current president of the International Primatological Society and his most recent awards and fellowships include the Baron-von-Swaine Award (University of Würzburg, 2000), American Academy of Arts and Sciences, Fellow (1993), Royal Anthropological Institute, Rivers Medal (1993).

item.title: Richard S. Grossman
item.description:
Richard S Grossman

Associate of IQSS
Andrews Professor of Economics, Wesleyan University
laptop_windows Website
  • Twitter

Richard S. Grossman is Andrews Professor of Economics at Wesleyan University in Middletown, CT and a Visiting Scholar at the Institute for Quantitative Social Science at Harvard University. He is the author of Unsettled Account: The Evolution of Banking in the Industrialized World Since 1800 (Princeton University Press, 2010) and WRONG: Nine Economic Policy Disasters and What We Can Learn from Them (Oxford University Press, 2013). He teaches classes in American and European Economic History, Macroeconomics, and Money and Banking. He graduated magna cum laude from Harvard College, holds an MScEcon degree in Economic History from the London School of Economics and Political Science of the University of London, and A.M. and PhD degrees in Economics from Harvard University. Prior to joining the faculty at Wesleyan, he worked as an international economist at the United States Department of State.  He has held visiting faculty appointments at Harvard, Yale, and the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, and has received research support from the National Science Foundation and the German Marshall Fund of the United States, and was a 2013 Fellow of the John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation. He has worked and consulted on Wall Street and has testified as an expert witness in federal court. He has been published in the American Economic Review, Journal of Economic History, Journal of Money, Credit, and Banking, and Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis, and other scholarly journals. His op-eds have appeared in the Wall Street Journal, Boston Globe, USA Today, and other newspapers and he has appeared on local, national, and foreign radio and TV. He is a Research Fellow at the Centre for Economic Policy Research in London and a Research Network Fellow of CESifo in Munich. He is a member of the International Advisory Board of the Queen’s University Centre for Economic History, Belfast, and an Advisor and Research Steering Group member at the Irish Centre for Economics, Policy, and History.

item.title: Reshmaan N Hussam
item.description:
Reshmaan N Hussam

Assistant Professor of Business Administration
email rhussam@hbs.edu
Reshmaan Hussam is an assistant professor of business administration in the Business, Government and International Economy Unit, teaching the Business, Government and International Economy course to MBA students. Her research explores questions at the intersection of development, behavioral, and health economics. Considering the puzzle of the ubiquitously low adoption of many low cost, high return goods, behaviors, and technologies in the developing world, she explores the role of learning and habit formation in sustained behavioral change. She also examines how to utilize community information to optimally allocate capital to microentrepreneurs as well as how digitization of financial services impacts financial inclusion in resource-poor settings.

item.title: Reed Rasband
item.description:
Reed Rasband

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email rasband@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Reed Rasband is a PhD Candidate at Harvard's Department of Government, studying comparative politics. He studies identity politics, public opinion and intergroup relations in the Global South, with an emphasis on migration within Latin America. His work incorporates a variety of methodological approaches, including survey data, interviews, and experiments. His dissertation investigates how Venezuelan migration has influenced relations between citizens and migrants in Chile and Brazil. He has also carried out research in Colombia, Thailand, and the United Kingdom.

His research tries to combine insights from psychology with quantitative methods of economic science in order to answer questions of social or political relevance. His recent projects study the role of audience effects on the public display of social preferences, and the psychology of human-AI interactions.

item.title: Rand Kiessig
item.description:
Rand Kiessig

Manager, IQSS Computer Lab Services
email rkiessig@iq.harvard.edu

Rand Kiessig joined IQSS in May 2012 and has led IQSS Computer Lab Services since 2015. A California native, Rand has lived across the U.S. — from Washington State to North Carolina to Massachusetts — with stints in Mexico and Spain along the way. Before coming to Harvard, he held a range of technical roles at organizations including Microsoft, Verio, Data General, and Iron Mountain.

item.title: Ramona Pop
item.description:
Ramona Pop

LISH Director, D^3 HBS
email rpop@hbs.edu

Dr. Pop currently serves as  Director in the Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard (LISH) l at the D^3 Institute within Harvard Business School. In this capacity, alongside Prof Karim Lakhani and a team of investigators, researchers and collaborators, Dr. Pop is overseeing and contributing to the ongoing scientific projects and research initiatives within the laboratory and D^3.  Her efforts are dedicated to supporting scientific and research activities that specifically address the integration and impact of artificial intelligence in business and society.  Prior to assuming the role at LISH/D^3, Dr. Pop conducted research at FAS within two stem cell laboratories. Notably, in Doug Melton lab at Harvard, she performed research aimed at developing stem cell therapies for Type 1 diabetes. Dr. Pop brings a wealth of experience to her current position, boasting two decades of research expertise in natural sciences.

item.description:
Ramana Nanda

Co-Director of Private Capital Project, Harvard Business School
Sarofim-Rock Professor of Business Administration, Harvard Business School
email rnanda@hbs.edu
laptop_windows Website

Ramana Nanda is the Sarofim-Rock Professor of Business Administration and Co-Director of the Private Capital Project at Harvard Business School. His research examines financing frictions facing new ventures, with an aim to help entrepreneurs with fundraising and to shed light on how financial intermediaries, corporate R&D and policy makers can improve the odds of selecting and commercializing the most promising ideas and technologies in the economy.

item.title: Rajarshi Mukherjee
item.description:

Assistant Professor, Department of Biostatistics
email ram521@mail.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Rajarshi Mukherjee's research is on causal inference from observational studies using non-parametric methods, sparse signal detection problems in genomics, and networks analysis with applications in neural spike train analysis.

item.title: Rafaella Sadun
item.description:
Rafaella Sadun

Professor, Harvard Business School
email rsadun@hbs.edu

Raffaella Sadun is a Professor at the Harvard Business School. Professor Sadun's research focuses on the economics of productivity, management and organizational change. Her research documents the economic and cultural determinants of managerial choices, as well as their implications for organizational performance in both the private and public sector (including healthcare and education). She is among the founders of the World Management Survey (www.worldmanagementsurvey.org) and the Executive Time Use Study (www.executivetimeuse.org). Professor Sadun's work has appeared in leading peer reviewed journals including the American Economic Review, the Quarterly Journal of Economics and the Economic Journal, and has been featured in the business press, including The New York Times, The Economist, The Wall Street Journal, and the Financial Times. She is a Faculty Research Fellow at the National Bureau of Economic Research, Faculty Associate at the Centre for Economic Performance at the London School of Economics, Research Affiliate at the Center for Economic Policy Research and Research Associate in the Ariadne Labs Program in the Department of Health Policy and Management at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. In 2012 Professor Sadun was nominated as a Junior Faculty Fellow at the Kauffman Foundation.

item.title: Rachel Franklin
item.description:
Rachel Franklin

Executive Director of the Center for Geographic Analysis
email rachel_franklin@cga.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Dr. Rachel Franklin started as Executive Director of the CGA in March of 2025, bringing more than 20 years’ experience as an educator and researcher in GIS, spatial analysis, and quantitative methods. Her research interests are in the sources and impacts of demographic change as it occurs at multiple spatial scales, and in the use of novel forms of data and analysis to identify and address that change. Her most recent research explores how smart city technologies contribute to and reinforce socio-economic and spatial inequalities. Her recent projects have examined regional inequality, population loss, and shrinkage at the local and regional scales, and her project titled “Spatial Inequality and the Smart City” focuses on improving the adoption of “smart city” technologies.

Senior AI and Data Executive

laptop_windows LinkedIn

Ra'ad Siraj is a senior data and AI executive with more than 30 years of experience building and scaling enterprise data, AI, and technology programs in regulated industries. He is known for deploying AI at scale while embedding governance and accountability as operational infrastructure — not compliance overhead.

item.title: Queenie Luo
item.description:
Queenie Luo

Data Scientist, IQSS Fellow, China Biographical Database Project
Ph.D. Candidate, Department of East Asian Languages and Civilizations
email queenieluo@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Queenie Luo graduated summa cum laude from Columbia University with a B.A. in Religion and Physics, and completed her M.T.S in Buddhist Studies at Harvard Divinity School. Before coming to Columbia, Queenie immersed herself in the Fashion Industry. At age nineteen, she became the youngest designer to have couture collections presented at Vancouver Fashion Week and went on to spend three years studying fashion design at Parsons School of Design in New York. In 2018, Queenie received the Phi Beta Kappa Prize, which is awarded to the candidate who best represents the ideals of the society—intellectual integrity, tolerance for other views, and a broad range of academic interests. Her current research is situated at the intersection of religion and science.

Qi Liu

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email qi_liu@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Personal website

Qi Liu is a PhD student in the department of Government at Harvard University. Her research interests are in international relations and political methodology, with a focus on international political economy and international institutions. She is especially interested in how states cooperate within the international regime complex. Prior to coming to Harvard, Qi earned a B.A. in Economics and International Relations from Peking University and an M.A. in Political Science from Duke University.

item.title: Priyanka Sethy
item.description:
Priyanka Sethy

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email psethy@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Priyanka is a PhD student in Comparative Politics. She focuses on social identity politics, race and ethnicity in politics, and immigrants and immigration. Her current projects include studying how diaspora communities engage with the politics of their origin countries, how natives form attitudes towards immigrants, and how immigrants form preferences around immigration.

item.title: Priya Shanmugam
item.description:
Priya Shanmugam

Department of Economics, GSAS
email priyavshanmugam@fas.harvard.edu
Priya is a second-year Ph.D. student in the Department of Economics. She is broadly interested in labor ecoNomics and the ecoNomics of social networks. Current projects include the heuristic basis of decision fatigue using data from the New Hampshire prison parole system, the role of local natural resource booms and busts on educational attainment behavior, and the mechanism of peer effects in medical treatment settings. Priya earned her B.A. in Economics from Dartmouth College in 2013.

Graduate Student, Program in Health Policy
location_on CGIS Knafel K311

Prachi Sanghavi is a 3rd year PhD student in the Program in Health Policy at the Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, and in the Evaluative Science and Statistics concentration of the program. She is interested in a range of subjects around violence and injuries, socio-ecoNomic determinants of health, and the roles of corporations in healthcare decisions. Her dissertation will explore the cost-effectiveness of different techNologies used in pre-hospital care. Prior to coming to Harvard, Prachi researched fire-related deaths among women in India, which are often related with domestic violence. Prachi graduated from Cornell University with a B.S. in Electrical and Computer Engineering, and has spent a few years as a management consultant in New York City. She is the recipient of a National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship.
